Description and Findings: A 10-syllable line of 5 rising feet ( $w S$ ). It is the dominant long line in English and Italian (Endecasillabo) traditions. It takes approx. 3.3s to recite, fitting the "human auditory present."
Usage Trends and Oeuvre Patterns: The staple of English poetry since the 15th century (Shakespeare, Milton, Pope, Wordsworth, Frost). In Russia, it gained ground in the mid-19th century for drama and philosophical lyrics (Pushkin, Tyutchev).
Metrical Divergences and Methodology: Common divergences include initial trochaic inversion, pyrrhic/spondaic substitution, and feminine (tailed) endings. Methodology involves "Stress Maximum" rules (Halle-Keyser), nPVI contrast analysis, and "Anatomical" scansion (Bones/Joints/Muscle).
Themes and Notable Citations: Associated with high-style narrative, drama, and meditation. Citations: Shakespeare, Sonnet 106 ("Of hand, of foot, of lip, of eye, of brow"); Milton,  Paradise Lost ; Dante,  Inferno  ("Nel mezzo del cammin...").

Description and Findings: A 4-foot binary meter ( $w S$ ). In Russian, it is the most historically prominent meter (Ya4), serving as a "national form" distinct from English/German versions due to frequent stress skipping.
Usage Trends and Oeuvre Patterns: The dominant meter of the Russian 18th-19th centuries (Pushkin, Lomonosov). Pushkin's  Eugene Onegin  established the standard. In English, it is often seen as "tighter" or associated with folk ditty and burlesque (Marvell, Housman).
Metrical Divergences and Methodology: Features "pyrrhic" substitutions (skipped stresses), especially on the 2nd and 3rd feet in Russian. Methodology leverages Taranovsky's law of regressive accentual dissimilation and statistical "stressing profiles."
Themes and Notable Citations: Often "neutral" or used for realistic narrative and melancholy love. Citations: Pushkin,  Eugene Onegin ; Lomonosov,  Oda na vzjatie Xotina ; Byron, "She walks in beauty."

Description and Findings: A 4-foot falling meter ( $S w$ ). It is characterized by high rhythmic energy and a "jigging" or incantatory quality.
Usage Trends and Oeuvre Patterns: Globally tied to song genres, folk-style imitations, and children's rhymes (Longfellow, Blake). In Russia, it is a staple for fairy tales (Pushkin, Ershov) and was the national meter of Estonia.
Metrical Divergences and Methodology: Frequently "catalectic" (missing the final slack syllable). Methodology tracks the "profile of stress" and utilizes nPVI to calculate rhythmic contrast.
Themes and Notable Citations: Themes of nature, folklore, and "energy/vitality." Citations: Blake, "The Tyger" ("Tyger! Tyger! burning bright"); Longfellow,  Hiawatha ; Pushkin, "The storm covers the sky."

Description and Findings: A non-classical meter with variable inter-ictic intervals (usually 1–2 syllables). It occupies a middle ground between syllabo-tonics and pure accentual (tonic) verse.
Usage Trends and Oeuvre Patterns: Dominant in Russian 20th-century poetry (Blok, Akhmatova, Gumilev, Brodsky). Derived from German tonic verse (Heine). In English, it is found in popular ballads and nursery rhymes (Frost, Blake).
Metrical Divergences and Methodology: Features "quasi-caesuras" and variable anacrusis. Methodology: "Russian Method" (statistical profiles of stress) and automated recognition using systems like "Treeton."
Themes and Notable Citations: Associated with lyrical, intimate, or supernatural themes. Citations: Blok, "I enter dark temples..."; Frost, "The Road Not Taken"; Heine, "Ein Fichtenbaum steht einsam."

Description and Findings: An ancient 6-foot meter of dactyls (— ⌣ ⌣) and spondees (— —). Traditionally the "epic" meter of Greek and Latin antiquity.
Usage Trends and Oeuvre Patterns: Standard for epic/philosophical poetry (Homer, Virgil). Adapted into German (Klopstock, Goethe) and Russian (Trediakovsky, Fet). Rare in English (Longfellow).
Metrical Divergences and Methodology: Methodology involves quantitative-syllabic scansion (long/short equivalencies). Often features a "penthemimeral" caesura and "bucolic diaeresis."
Themes and Notable Citations: Themes of heroism, antiquity, and the sublime. Citations: Homer,  Iliad ; Virgil,  Aeneid ; Klopstock,  Messiah ; Longfellow,  Evangeline .

Description and Findings: A 14-line fixed form typically in iambic pentameter. Major variants include Italian (Petrarchan: 8+6) and English (Shakespearean: 4+4+4+2).
Usage Trends and Oeuvre Patterns: Astonishing longevity (700 years). Historically associated with love, but adapted for social critique and philosophical meditation. Prominent in Russian Silver Age (Ivanov, Hodasevich).
Metrical Divergences and Methodology: Structured around the "turn" or  volta . Methodology tracks rhyme schemes (e.g.,  $abbaabba$  vs.  $ababcdcdefefgg$ ) and utilizes dependency parses for enjambment study.
Themes and Notable Citations: Themes of unrequited love, time, and mortality. Citations: Shakespeare, Sonnet 18; Keats, "On First Looking into Chapman's Homer"; Milton, "When I consider how my light is spent."

Description and Findings: A 12-syllable line (dodecasyllable) with a mandatory central caesura. The primary meter of French Classicism and the German Baroque.
Usage Trends and Oeuvre Patterns: Usage declined in Russia from 16% in the 18th century to nearly vanishing in the Soviet era. Revived in modernism for archaic stylization (Grünbein).
Metrical Divergences and Methodology: In French, requires stress on the 6th/12th syllables. Methodology involves calculating distribution of masculine/dactylic endings at the caesura.
Themes and Notable Citations: Associated with formal grandeur and courtly erudition. Citations: Racine,  Phèdre ; Hugo, "Je suis banni!"; Ronsard; Boileau.

Description and Findings: Verse characterized by the absence of regular meter and rhyme, relying on syntactic phrasing, intonation, and line breaks for integrity.
Usage Trends and Oeuvre Patterns: Became the staple form in the 20th century (Whitman, Pound, Eliot, Williams). In Russia, it increased significantly from the 1960s (Kuzmin, Khlebnikov).
Metrical Divergences and Methodology: Methodology focuses on "line-syntax," "prolongation," and "rhythmical isolation." Analyzed via "Phrasal Scansion" and dependency grammar.
Themes and Notable Citations: Mimics internal monologue or "stream of consciousness." Citations: Whitman,  Leaves of Grass ; Pound, "In a Station of the Metro"; Eliot,  The Waste Land .

Description and Findings: A tonic system where only the number of stresses (accents) is regulated, regardless of syllable count. Native to Old English poetry.
Usage Trends and Oeuvre Patterns: Prominent in folk poetry, nursery rhymes, and modern works by Mayakovsky (e.g., 4-stress accented verse) and T.S. Eliot.
Metrical Divergences and Methodology: Methodology involves counting beats and identifying "silent beats" or musical rests in performance. Includes "Stress-timed" rhythm analysis.
Themes and Notable Citations: Associated with ritualistic, "unconscious," or choral effects. Citations:  Beowulf ; Langland,  Piers Plowman ; Mayakovsky, "Chetyre. Tyazhelye, kak udar."

Description and Findings: Meters where stresses occur every three syllables. Generally noted for higher rhythmic regularity compared to binary meters.
Usage Trends and Oeuvre Patterns: Amphibrachs are historically linked to the ballad genre in Russia (Nekrasov, Zhukovsky). Anapests are associated with speed, joy, or "social suffering."
Metrical Divergences and Methodology: Methodology tracks the probability of stress distribution and word-boundary (caesura) placement. Omissions of stress are rarer than in iambs.
Themes and Notable Citations: Citations: Nekrasov,  Frost, Red Nose ; Byron, "Destruction of Sennacherib"; Mandelstam, "For the rattling valor...".

Description and Findings: A complex metrical system where feet start with one stress and contain a variable number of "slack" syllables (0 to 4).
Usage Trends and Oeuvre Patterns: The defining innovation of Gerard Manley Hopkins. Suggested as suitable for Indian English poetry and similar to Finnish stress-based meters.
Metrical Divergences and Methodology: Features "outrides" (extrametrical syllables) and "overreaving." Methodology utilizes stochastic maxent grammars and two-tiered scansion.
Themes and Notable Citations: Associated with spiritual "inscape" and divine revelation. Citations: Hopkins, "The Windhover" and "The Wreck of the Deutschland."

Description and Findings: An 8-line stanza form ( $abababcc$ ) traditionally in iambic pentameter. A closed poetic system.
Usage Trends and Oeuvre Patterns: Primary form for Italian epic (Ariosto, Tasso). Adapted by Byron for irony and Pushkin ( Little House in Kolomna ) for stylistic experimentation.
Metrical Divergences and Methodology: Analyzed via macrosyntactic "pause strength" (Tomashevsky 0–3 scale). English octaves are noted as more "talkative" with fewer internal stops.
Themes and Notable Citations: Associated with high-epic themes or ironic "parlando" digressions. Citations: Ariosto,  Orlando Furioso ; Byron,  Don Juan ; Pushkin,  Little House in Kolomna .

Description and Findings: A quatrain form alternating tetrameter and trimeter lines ( $a4b3c4b3$  or  $4343$ ).
Usage Trends and Oeuvre Patterns: Dominant in English folk songs, hymns (Isaac Watts), and the works of Emily Dickinson.
Metrical Divergences and Methodology: Often features a "silent" or "virtual" fourth beat in shorter lines to maintain a  $4 \times 4$  structure. Methodology tracks "parallelism" and "saliency."
Themes and Notable Citations: Narrative vignettes, often tragic or supernatural. Citations: Dickinson, "In lands I never saw..."; "Sir Patrick Spens"; "Lord Arlen."

Description and Findings: A 19-line fixed form ( $5$  tercets +  $1$  quatrain) with two alternating refrains.
Usage Trends and Oeuvre Patterns: Widely used in the 20th century (Dylan Thomas, Roethke, Bishop).
Metrical Divergences and Methodology: Rigidly defined by line-level repetition tracking. GPT-4 frameworks show high accuracy in detection.
Themes and Notable Citations: Thematic association with loss, persistence, and self-discipline. Citations: Dylan Thomas, "Do Not Go Gentle into That Good Night."

Description and Findings: A tonic verse form with intervals between stresses typically ranging from 1 to 3 syllables. Intermediate between Dolnik and Accentual verse.
Usage Trends and Oeuvre Patterns: Found in 20th-century Russian poetry (Blok, Mayakovsky, Rozhdestvensky) and underlying Russian folk epics (Bylinas).
Metrical Divergences and Methodology: Methodology involves counting inter-ictal intervals (e.g.,  $3n+1$ ). Requires vertical analysis of whole texts to distinguish from Dolnik.
Themes and Notable Citations: Associated with archaic/folk styles and experimental modernism. Citations: Blok, "I was all in colorful rags..."; Mayakovsky, "Conversation with a tax inspector...".

Description and Findings: Verse measured solely by syllable count, disregarding fixed stress positions.
Usage Trends and Oeuvre Patterns: Prevalent in 17th-early 18th century Russia (Simeon Polotsky). Experimented with by Modernists (Marianne Moore, Auden).
Metrical Divergences and Methodology: Methodology involves numbering syllables and tracking elision/phonetic spelling. Suppresses the difference between accented/unaccented units.
Themes and Notable Citations: Associated with visual design and understated dignity. Citations: Polotsky,  The Garden of Many Flowers ; Moore, "The Paper Nautilus."

Description and Findings: A foot of two unstressed syllables ( $x x$ ). In syllabo-tonic systems, it is treated as a substitution for iambic or trochaic feet.
Usage Trends and Oeuvre Patterns: Trediakovsky and Sumarokov justified its use as a necessity for the long words in the Russian language.
Metrical Divergences and Methodology: Defined as the omission of an obligatory metric stress. Algorithms account for schematic omissions of emphasis.
Themes and Notable Citations: Used to provide "greater variety" in Russian binary meters. Citations: Pushkin,  Eugene Onegin .

Description and Findings: A foot of two stressed syllables ( $/ /$ ). Genuine spondees are difficult to achieve in German/English due to multisyllabic stress nature.
Usage Trends and Oeuvre Patterns: Used for emphasis, weight, or to slow a line (Milton, Yeats, Pound).
Metrical Divergences and Methodology: Often replaced by trochees in latitudinarian practice. Some theorists (Attridge) argue they contain an "implied offbeat."
Themes and Notable Citations: Associated with weight, slowness, and gravity. Citations: Milton ("Rocks, caves, lakes..."); Klopstock, "Sponda."

Description and Findings: The non-coincidence of poetic and syntactic boundaries; the running-over of a sentence into the next line.
Usage Trends and Oeuvre Patterns: Historically frequent in Baroque, Romanticism, and Modernism. Re-emerged in sharp forms in Brodsky and Björling.
Metrical Divergences and Methodology: Categorized as  rejet ,  contre-rejet , and  double . Methodology uses dependency grammar to measure the strength of the broken link.
Themes and Notable Citations: Creates tension between syntax and line break. Citations: Batyushkov ("I gordyy um ne pobedit..."); James Wright ("I would break / Into blossom").

Description and Findings: A fundamental principle where units are connected by echoing, contrasting, or "seconding."
Usage Trends and Oeuvre Patterns: Universal in Biblical Hebrew poetry, Old English verse, and oral traditions (Kalevala).
Metrical Divergences and Methodology: Involves identifying "couplets of couplets" and "strophes" aligned with syntactic boundaries. Jakobsonian styleme analysis.
Themes and Notable Citations: Associated with emphatic restatement and causality. Citations: Psalm 94; Whitman, "When I Heard the Learn’d Astronomer."

Description and Findings: An archaic Roman verse form ( $7 + 6$  or  $6 + 7$  syllables). Its exact nature (quantitative vs. accentual) is disputed.
Usage Trends and Oeuvre Patterns: Used by Livius Andronicus and Naevius. Often seen as a "clumsy" predecessor to the hexameter.
Metrical Divergences and Methodology: Theories include "mora-counting" and "stress-based" groupings. Typically divided into two  cola  with a caesura.
Themes and Notable Citations: Early Roman inscriptions and epic translations. Citations: "Malum dabunt Metelli Naevio poetae."

Description and Findings: A light verse form of 5 lines rhyming  $aabba$ . Lines 1, 2, 5 are trimeter; 3, 4 are dimeter.
Usage Trends and Oeuvre Patterns: Obeys "Music's Rule of Three." T.S. Eliot utilized this form in "Gus: The Theatre Cat."
Metrical Divergences and Methodology: Analyzed as part of the "Victorian aesthetics of nonsense." Methodology involves identifying consequence in the final line.
Themes and Notable Citations: Humorous or light themes. Example: "There once was an old man from Esser...".

Description and Findings: A complex form of three 11-syllable lines and one 5-syllable Adonic line.
Usage Trends and Oeuvre Patterns: Revived during Russian Modernism (Ivanov). Common in Greek lyric (Sappho) and English imitations (Swinburne).
Metrical Divergences and Methodology: Historically quantitative; modern versions use stress-based scansion. Analyzed using "syllabo-metric" patterns.
Themes and Notable Citations: Associated with lyric emotion and mortality. Citations: Sappho, Ode II; Horace, Odes.

Description and Findings: A quantitative meter traditionally described as 9 moras per half-line.
Usage Trends and Oeuvre Patterns: Features alliteration and a central caesura. Usage shows a "compensatory relationship" between half-lines.
Metrical Divergences and Methodology: Heavy variation in mora count (6–15). Analyzed using mora-counting and quantitative iambic footing ( $H, LL, LH$ ).
Themes and Notable Citations: Notable author: Sayid Maxamad Cabdulle Xasan. Citation: "The Sayid’s Reply."

Description and Findings: A Persian/Arabic lyric form with a mono-rhyme scheme ( $aa ba ca...$ ).
Usage Trends and Oeuvre Patterns: Introduced by Fet. Kuzmin and Ivanov utilized it for orientalist aesthetics.
Metrical Divergences and Methodology: Uses the  Radif  (a repeating refrain word after the rhyme). Requires a sharp break to distinguish the refrain.
Themes and Notable Citations: Themes of love and wine. Citations: Kuzmin, "Wreath of Springs."

Description and Findings: An accentual verse of 11 syllables, with a mandatory stress on the 10th syllable.
Usage Trends and Oeuvre Patterns: The core of Dante's  Divine Comedy . Rhythmic variations are achieved through secondary accents and synalephe.
Metrical Divergences and Methodology: Methodology involves distinguishing between hiatus/diphthong and synalephe/dialephe. Includes  versi sdruccioli .
Themes and Notable Citations: Used for narrative and musical stylistic effects. Citation: "Nel mezzo del cammin di nostra vita."

Description and Findings: Traditionally a 5-7-5-7-7 mora structure.
Usage Trends and Oeuvre Patterns: A prominence-insensitive rhythm (no stress). Speakers adjust timing by 121–156ms to produce isochronous lengths.
Metrical Divergences and Methodology: Utilizes an "octomoraic template" where silence/pauses act as moras to regulate timing.
Themes and Notable Citations: Citation: Takuboku Ishikawa, "Love song to myself."

Description and Findings: A 39-line French form using a mathematical lexical rotation (615243) of six end-words instead of rhyme.
Usage Trends and Oeuvre Patterns: Popular Provençal form in the 20th century (Swinburne, Roethke).
Metrical Divergences and Methodology: Methodology involves numerological sequence mapping. Includes "double" and "obsessive" variants.
Themes and Notable Citations: Themes of obsession, circularity, or memory. Citations: Swinburne, "Sestina."
